C:/Users/frank/.platformio/packages/toolchain-xtensa-esp-elf/bin/../lib/gcc/xtensa-esp-elf/13.2.0/../../../../xtensa-esp-elf/bin/ld.exe: IRAM0 segment data does not fit.

ouch, we haven't even implemented half of the project yet
agent_reply: The mSBC undefined symbol is gone by changing [bluedroid_sbc.c](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/bluedroid/bluedroid_sbc.c) to use `OI_CODEC_SBC_DecoderReset(...)` and then set `mSBCEnabled = TRUE`, instead of calling the missing `OI_CODEC_mSBC_DecoderReset`.

The IRAM overflow was the ESP32 classic BT plus PSRAM combo. I disabled `BOARD_HAS_PSRAM` only for `[env:theflybringuptest]` in [platformio.ini](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/platformio.ini), which frees enough IRAM for this bringup test without changing the base env.
Also finished the previous cleanup: `print_bda` is gone from the BT tests; they use `log_bda`.

user_prompt: if I understand correctly, IRAM is the instructions that are always in IRAM and never fetched, so they have super low latency?
agent_reply: Yes, your understanding is basically right, with one small wording tweak.

IRAM is internal instruction RAM. Code placed there is loaded into RAM at boot and the CPU fetches it directly from internal RAM instead of fetching it through the SPI flash cache. That makes it fast and predictable, but more importantly on ESP32, some code must live there because the flash cache is sometimes unavailable, especially during flash/PSRAM/cache operations and certain interrupts.

Most normal firmware code does not live in IRAM. It lives in flash as `IROM`, and the CPU executes it through cache. That is usually fine, but it is not always available and it consumes no scarce IRAM.
1. Classic Bluetooth pulled in a lot of IRAM-resident controller/PHY/coexist code.
2. The M5Stack Core2 board definition also defines `BOARD_HAS_PSRAM`.
3. PSRAM support pulls in more cache/PSRAM support code that also needs IRAM.
4. Together, that overflowed the ESP32’s limited IRAM region by about `3960` bytes.
So the linker was saying: “I can fit your flash code fine, but the always-in-internal-RAM instruction section is too large.”
The fix I made was scoped to the bringup test env:
That stops the Arduino core from enabling PSRAM support for this test build, which freed enough IRAM. The base environment still has PSRAM.

There was a second unrelated issue too: our `bluedroid_sbc.c` called `OI_CODEC_mSBC_DecoderReset`, but our current library setup does not compile the decoder source file that defines that symbol. I replaced it with the regular decoder reset call plus manually setting the `mSBCEnabled` flag, which is what that helper does internally.

Longer term, the warning sign is real: “Classic BT + PSRAM + WiFi/web/display/everything” can get tight on original ESP32 IRAM. For production we may want slimmer envs, fewer always-linked modules, less debug build baggage, or a clean separation between bringup tests and the full app.

user_prompt: if our copy inside `lib\bluedroid\encoder` and `lib\bluedroid\decoder`, then block our own copy and use the included one from esp-idf
agent_reply: Done. I blocked our local `lib/bluedroid/encoder` and `lib/bluedroid/decoder` sources from compiling and switched the wrapper to use ESP-IDF’s included Bluedroid SBC symbols instead.
- `platformio.ini` now exposes the ESP-IDF BT/SBC include paths
- Removed the earlier local symbol-prefix workaround

I also fixed the wrapper for the ESP-IDF encoder shape, including copying PCM into `as16PcmBuffer` when `SBC_NO_PCM_CPY_OPTION` is false. That is likely the actual crash fix for the `EncPacking` StoreProhibited path.
- The map file now shows SBC encoder/decoder symbols coming from ESP-IDF `libbt.a`, while our library only contributes `bluedroid_sbc.c` as the adapter.

I also fixed the wrapper for the ESP-IDF encoder shape, including copying PCM into `as16PcmBuffer` when `SBC_NO_PCM_CPY_OPTION` is false. That is likely the actual crash fix for the `EncPacking` StoreProhibited path.
- The map file now shows SBC encoder/decoder symbols coming from ESP-IDF `libbt.a`, while our library only contributes `bluedroid_sbc.c` as the adapter.
